verses.text: From Paul, God’s willing slave {a} and an apostle of Jesus, the Anointed One, to Titus. {b} I’m writing you to further the faith {c} of God’s chosen ones and lead them to the full knowledge of the truth that leads to godliness,

footnotes.text: Or “bondservant.”
Although the name Titus is not found until v. 4, it is included here to enhance the understanding of Paul’s introduction.
Or “according to the faith of God’s elect.”

verses.text: which rests on the hope of eternal life. God, who never lies, has promised us this before time began.
In his own time he unveiled his word through the preaching of the gospel, which was entrusted to me by the command of God our Life Giver. {d}

footnotes.text: As translated from the Aramaic. The Greek is “Savior.”

verses.text: Titus, you are my true son in the faith we share. May grace and peace descend to you from God the Father and our Savior, the Anointed One, Jesus!

The reason I stationed you in Crete {e} was so that you could set things in order and complete what was left unfinished, {f} and to raise up and appoint church elders {g} in every city, just as I had instructed you.

footnotes.text: A Greek island in the Aegean Sea. Paul’s ship had stopped on the way to Rome at Fair Havens, a small harbor on the southern coast of Crete (Acts 27:7–12).
The unfinished work would be bringing believers into maturity in Christ and raising up godly, qualified leaders who could teach the church and lead it forward.

Or “ordain elders.” This is the Greek word presbyteros, which means “senior leaders.” These are church elders who would function as overseers, teachers, and shepherds of God’s flock. The same Greek word is used for women in 1 Tim. 5:2. Although generally assumed to be male in the cultural context of that day, there is nothing to indicate that presbyteros is gender exclusive. The church elder is called an “overseer” (or “bishop”) in v. 7, which indicates that both terms speak of the same office and are synonymous.

verses.text: Each of them must be above reproach, devoted solely to his wife, {h} whose children are believers and not rebellious or out of control.

footnotes.text: Or “the husband of one wife” or “married only once.”

verses.text: The overseer, since he serves God’s household, {i} must be someone of blameless character and not be opinionated or short-tempered. He must not be a drunkard or violent or greedy.

footnotes.text: Or “God’s steward.”

verses.text: Instead he should be one who is known for his hospitality and a lover of goodness. {j} He should be recognized as one who is fair-minded, pure-hearted, and self-controlled.

footnotes.text: The Aramaic can be translated “one who nurtures goodness” (in others). We would say, “one who brings out the best in others.”

verses.text: He must have a firm grasp of the trustworthy message that he has been taught. This will enable him to both encourage others with healthy teachings and provide convincing answers to those who oppose his message.
There are many wayward people, smooth talkers, and deceivers—especially the converts from Judaism. {k}

footnotes.text: Or “those of the circumcision” (group), i.e., Jewish converts. Paul is pointing to three types of people who will refute and argue with church leaders: rebels, empty talkers, and deceivers. The leaders (elders) must be faithful to the Scriptures in order to correct them and set them in order.

verses.text: They must be silenced {l} because they are disrupting entire families with their corrupt teachings, all for their dishonest greed.

footnotes.text: Or “reined in.” The Greek word epistomizo is used for the reins of a horse.

verses.text: A certain one of them, one of their own prophets, {m} said, “Those Cretans are nothing but liars, worthless beasts, and lazy gluttons.” {n}

footnotes.text: Although the Greek uses the word prophet, it is not used here in the biblical sense of a “prophet” of God, for the author of this proverb was a pagan.
A quote from the Oracles of Epimenides, a six-century BC poet. The first line is quoted from The Hymn to Zeus by Callimachus.

verses.text: He certainly knew what he was talking about! For this reason, correct them thoroughly so that their lives will line up with the truths of our faith.
Instruct them not to pay any attention to Jewish myths or follow the teachings of those who reject the truth.
It’s true that all is pure to those who have pure hearts, but to the corrupt unbelievers nothing is pure. Their minds and consciences are defiled.
They claim to know God, but by their actions they deny him. They are disgusting, disobedient, and disqualified from doing anything good.
Your duty is to teach them to embrace a lifestyle that is consistent with sound doctrine.
Lead the male elders {a} into disciplined lives full of dignity and self-control. Urge them to have a solid faith, generous love, and patient endurance.

footnotes.text: Or “old men.”

verses.text: Likewise with the female elders, {b} lead them into lives free from gossip and drunkenness and to be teachers of beautiful things. {c}

footnotes.text: Or “old women.”
As translated from the Aramaic. The Greek is “good things.”

verses.text: This will enable them to teach the younger women {d} to love their husbands, to love their children,

footnotes.text: Paul is contrasting the “elders” with the “younger” (Gr. neos) and could possibly be referring to those who are newly converted.

verses.text: and to be self-controlled and pure, taking care of their household and being devoted to {e} their husbands. By doing these things the word of God will not be discredited.

footnotes.text: Or “supportive of.”

verses.text: Likewise, guide the younger men into living disciplined lives for Christ.
Above all, set yourself apart as a model of a life nobly lived. With dignity, demonstrate integrity in all that you teach. {f}

footnotes.text: “Integrity in all that you teach” would imply serious study of God’s Word and its personal application in our lives, and not teaching impulsively from one’s opinion, which only leads to arguments and divisions. This kind of integrity gives someone the right to be heard.

verses.text: Bring a clear, wholesome message {g} that cannot be condemned, and then your critics will be embarrassed, with nothing bad to say about us. {h}

footnotes.text: Or “with sound speech.”
Paul and Timothy were a team. If one were to err, it would affect the other.

verses.text: Servants {i} are to be supportive of {j} their masters and do what is pleasing in every way. They are not to be argumentative

footnotes.text: Or “bondservants.”
Or “submitted to.”

verses.text: nor steal {k} but prove themselves to be completely loyal and trustworthy. By doing this they will advertise {l} through all that they do the beautiful teachings of God our Savior.

footnotes.text: Businesses today lose millions of dollars to employee theft. Believers are to be meticulously honest in the workplace.
Or “adorn [beautify] the doctrine of God.”

verses.text: God’s marvelous grace {m} has manifested in person, bringing salvation for everyone. {n}

footnotes.text: Grace extends God’s kindness and love to us every moment and makes us “worthy” of his acceptance. Grace is unconditional, unmerited, indescribable favor from God.
That is, grace has revealed a salvation available for everyone. Or “God’s marvelous grace has appeared to all, bringing salvation.”

verses.text: This same grace teaches us how to live each day as we turn our backs on ungodliness and indulgent lifestyles, {o} and it equips us to live self-controlled, upright, godly lives in this present age. {p}

footnotes.text: The Greek word for “ungodliness” is singular, while the word for “indulgent lifestyles” is plural. This has led some scholars to believe that we are to turn our backs on both the root principle of ungodliness and the specific acts that result from ungodliness.
These three adjectives—“self-controlled,” “upright,” and “godly”—refer to our behavior, our behavior toward others and toward God.

verses.text: For we continue to look forward to the joyful fulfillment of our hope in the dawning splendor {q} of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus, the Anointed One. {r}

footnotes.text: The Greek word is epiphaneia (epiphany) and is a nominalized verb that means “a brightness shining all around.” It was through epiphaneia, the beautiful appearing of Christ as a baby, that a wonderful hope was brought to all the world.

Or “our great God and our Savior, Jesus Christ.” Note the four great truths of grace in vv. 11–13: (1) Grace is a person—“our great God and Savior, Jesus, the Anointed One.” (2) Grace brings salvation for all. (3) Grace educates us on how to live pure lives. (4) Grace brings a hope of the manifestation (appearing) of Christ. This is a hope worth waiting for.

verses.text: He sacrificed himself for us that he might purchase our freedom from every lawless deed and to purify for himself a people who are his very own, {s} passionate to do what is beautiful in his eyes.

footnotes.text: We are a people encircled by God himself. The compound Greek word periousios is translated from “around,” as a circle, and the verb “to be.” It can mean something surrounded by something. It can be charted by a dot within a circle. As the circle surrounds the dot, so God is around each one of his saints. The circle has the dot all to itself. So God has his very own all to himself. We are unique in that we belong only to him. Uniquely his, we are monopolized by God, taken into himself by grace through faith and surrounded by his love.

verses.text: So preach these truths and exhort others to follow them. Be willing to expose sin in order to bring correction with full authority, {t} without being intimidated {u} by anyone.

footnotes.text: Or “Speak these things; exhort or rebuke [speak in order to expose sin and bring correction] with all authority.”
Or “disregarded.”

verses.text: Remind people to respect {a} their governmental leaders on every level as law-abiding citizens and to be ready to fulfill their civic duty.

footnotes.text: Or “be subject to.”

verses.text: And remind them to never tear down anyone with their words or quarrel, {b} but instead be considerate, humble, and courteous to everyone.

footnotes.text: Or “strive with others.” The implication is that we accept the differences of others and allow people to be who they are and not try to make them over into our image of who we think they should be.

verses.text: For it wasn’t that long ago that we behaved foolishly in our stubborn disobedience. We were easily led astray as slaves to worldly passions and pleasures. We wasted our lives in doing evil, and with hateful jealousy we hated others.
When the extraordinary compassion of God our Savior {c} and his overpowering love suddenly appeared in person, as the brightness of a dawning day, {d}

footnotes.text: Many scholars believe that vv. 4–7 are ancient Christian poetry or perhaps the words to a hymn.
Implied in the Greek word epiphainō (epiphany), which means “to shine forth [brightly] in an appearing.”

verses.text: he came to save us. Not because of any virtuous deed that we have done but only because of his extravagant mercy.
He saved us, resurrecting us {e} through the washing of rebirth. We are made completely new by the Holy Spirit, {f} whom he splashed over us richly by Jesus, the Messiah, our Life Giver.

footnotes.text: As translated from the Aramaic.
All three members of the Trinity are mentioned in vv. 4–6 and are seen as active participants in our salvation.

verses.text: So as a gift of his love, and since we are faultless— innocent before his face— we can now become heirs of all things, all because of an overflowing hope of eternal life.

How true and faithful is this message! I want you to especially emphasize {g} these truths, so that those who believe in God will be careful to devote themselves to doing good works. It is always beautiful and profitable for believers to do good works.

footnotes.text: Or “affirm strongly,” a hapax legomenon.

verses.text: But avoid useless controversies, {h} genealogies, {i} pointless quarrels, and arguments over the law, which will get you nowhere. {j}

footnotes.text: The Aramaic can be translated “offensive debates.”
The Aramaic can be translated “tribal traditions.”
See also Heb. 13:9.

verses.text: After a first and second warning, have nothing more to do with a divisive person who refuses to be corrected.
For you know that such a one is entwined with his sin and stands self-condemned.
When I send Artemas {k} or Tychicus {l} to you, be sure to meet me at the City of Victory, {m} for I’ve decided to spend the winter there.

footnotes.text: Artemas, or “Artemas of Lystra,” was considered to be one of the seventy disciples whom Jesus sent out.

Tychichus, an Ephesian, was a beloved coworker of Paul and is mentioned five times in the New Testament (here; Acts 20:4; Eph. 6:21; Col. 4:7; 2 Tim. 4:12). He was listed among the seventy disciples whom Jesus sent out according to Hippolytus of Rome. See Francis Mershman, “St. Tychicus” (1913) Catholic Encyclopedia, Charles Herbermann, editor.
Or “Nicopolis,” a Greek city on the western shore. Nicopolis means “the City of Victory.”

verses.text: Give a generous send-off to Zenas the scribe {n} and Apollos, {o} and send them on their journey with what they need.

footnotes.text: Or “lawyer.” The word translated “lawyer” can be used for either Greek or Roman law. Zenas is considered to be one of the seventy whom Jesus sent out.

Apollos was a powerful preacher and coworker of Paul, who was very influential in the church of Corinth. He is listed ten times in the New Testament (Acts 18:24; 19:1; 1 Cor. 1:12; 3:4–6, 22; 4:6; 16:12). Jerome states that Apollos, after Paul’s letters brought healing to the divisions of the church in Corinth, returned and became an elder (overseer) in the church. See Jerome, Commentary on the Epistle of Titus.

verses.text: Encourage the believers to be passionately devoted to beautiful works of righteousness by meeting the urgent needs of others and not be unfruitful.
Everyone here with me sends their loving greetings to you. Greet the believers who love us in the faith. May God’s wonderful grace be with you all! Love in Christ, Paul {p}

footnotes.text: The Aramaic adds, “The end of the letter written by Paul to Titus from Nicopolis, sent by the hand of Zenas and Apollos.”
